retrieved_docs.title: Tooth Fracture -- Treatment / Management -- Root Fractures
retrieved_docs.content: The mobile segment must be stabilized with a flexible, passive splint for up to 4 weeks, with an extended splinting period for fractures in the cervical 3rd. Fracture healing should be monitored at follow-up visits after 4 weeks when the splint is removed, then at 6 to 8 weeks, 4 months, 6 months, 1 year, and annually for at least 5 years.

retrieved_docs.title: Splinting -- Technique or Treatment -- Splinting Technique
retrieved_docs.content: Ensure adequate analgesia before splint application to promote muscle relaxation and facilitate fracture reduction.

retrieved_docs.title: Splinting -- Technique or Treatment -- Splinting Technique
retrieved_docs.content: Reduce any fracture by restoring the bone length, rotation, and alignment utilizing radiographic confirmation as necessary.

retrieved_docs.title: Trauma to the Primary Dentition -- Treatment / Management -- Root Fractures
retrieved_docs.content: If the coronal fragment has been repositioned and splinted, a clinical follow-up examination should be done after one week, four weeks (for splint removal), eight weeks, and one year. A clinical exam after one year is recommended if the coronal fragment has been extracted. Radiographic follow-up is only indicated where clinical findings are suggestive of pathosis.

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Emergencies -- Treatment / Management -- Tooth Fractures
retrieved_docs.content: The initial focus for treating root fractures involves ensuring appropriate crown fragment placement on physical exams and radiography. The crown is often mobile and thus needs to be stabilized with a dental splint for weeks to months, with cervically-located fractures requiring a longer duration of immobilization when compared to apically located fractures. [49]

retrieved_docs.title: EMS Immobilization Techniques -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Emergency medical services (EMS) personnel continue to be the first-line responders in dealing with the majority out of hospital emergencies including trauma situations. The ATLS guidelines ( advanced trauma life support) which have been developed in the eighties continue to be the gold standards in assessing and setting priorities in the management of life-threatening injuries in a time-efficient and logical manner. Immobilization of the spine has been an essential part of the teaching in addition to pelvic binders and splinting of long bone fractures. Different medical types of equipment have been developed to allow effectiveness and ease of application in addition to allowing flexibility and vital access for the management of airway and other procedures.

retrieved_docs.title: Splinting -- Technique or Treatment -- Splinting Technique
retrieved_docs.content: Ensure that any soft tissue injuries are addressed before splint placement.

retrieved_docs.title: Tooth Fracture -- Treatment / Management -- Root Fractures
retrieved_docs.content: Initial management of root fractures involves repositioning the displaced crown fragment and confirming proper alignment with an x-ray. Endodontic treatment should not be performed during the emergency appointment. The crown fragment should not be removed because fractures at the cervical level can heal.

retrieved_docs.title: Splinting -- Technique or Treatment -- Splinting Technique
retrieved_docs.content: Activate the splinting material by saturating them in the water bucket. Press the sheets together to laminate them for increased strength and adhesion.

retrieved_docs.title: Splinting -- Review Questions
retrieved_docs.content: Access free multiple choice questions on this topic. Click here for a simplified version. Comment on this article.

retrieved_docs.title: Cervical Radiculopathy -- Treatment / Management -- Conservative Management
retrieved_docs.content: An aggressive, well-designed physical therapy program can provide significant relief. In the setting of surgical intervention, physical therapy can speed recovery. Using a cervical pillow at nighttime can help alleviate symptoms and make sleeping easier during recovery. Short-term use of a soft cervical collar can provide some relief.

retrieved_docs.title: Splinting -- Technique or Treatment -- Splinting Technique
retrieved_docs.content: Apply a stockinette circumferentially to the injured area extending proximal and distal of the splinted region.

retrieved_docs.title: Tooth Fracture -- Treatment / Management -- Crown-Root Fractures
retrieved_docs.content: Clinical and radiographic follow-up examinations should be conducted at 1 week, 6 to 8 weeks, 3 months, 6 months, and 1 year. After the 1st year, annual evaluations should continue for a minimum of 5 years.

retrieved_docs.title: EMS Long Spine Board Immobilization -- Contraindications
retrieved_docs.content: A relative contraindication in patients with penetrating trauma to the head, neck, or torso without neurologic deficit or complaint. [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Trauma to the Primary Dentition -- Treatment / Management -- Root Fractures
retrieved_docs.content: If the coronal fragment is displaced, excessively mobile, and interfering with occlusion, there are two options: a) extract the loose coronal fragment only, leaving the apical fragment in place to be reabsorbed; or b) gently reposition the loose coronal fragment. If the fragment is unstable in its new position, stabilize it with a flexible splint attached to adjacent uninjured teeth for four weeks.

retrieved_docs.title: Splinting -- Contraindications
retrieved_docs.content: While no specific contraindications to splinting exist, certain injuries and patient-specific comorbidities require special consideration.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 797
retrieved_docs.content: particular bones) are nested under a universal category of late effects, which also encompasses non‑fracture injuries such as burns and frostbite. Recognizing these relationships aids clinicians in systematically assessing and managing the long‑term sequelae of diverse traumatic injuries.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1736
retrieved_docs.content: Traction, splints, and other wound‑care modalities are understood to have a broader relationship with the term “الجر,” the Arabic word for traction, indicating that these interventions are encompassed within the larger concept of traction in wound management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 2 -- Community 1057
retrieved_docs.content: grouped under a unified late‑effect classification that is further subdivided by anatomical region. This structured relationship aids clinicians in linking acute injury diagnoses with their potential long‑term sequelae and facilitates comprehensive patient management across different injury types and body regions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2430
retrieved_docs.content: Other miscellaneous procedures are classified as a subset of other nonoperative procedures, indicating that they fall under the broader category of nonoperative interventions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 1 -- Community 189
retrieved_docs.content: relationships form a structured network in which fractures—whether of multiple bones, the spine, trunk, limbs, femoral neck, or skull vault—are intricately linked to specific late‑effect conditions, anatomical locations, treatment morphologies, broader injury classifications, and associated behavioral factors, providing a comprehensive framework for understanding fracture sequelae and related injuries in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 2111
retrieved_docs.content: Cervical vertebra fracture (كسر في الفقرة الرقبية) is classified within the broader category of other fractures, and it frequently co‑occurs with pelvic fracture (كسر الحوض). Recognizing that a cervical vertebra fracture belongs to the group of other fractures and that it often appears alongside a pelvic fracture can guide clinicians to assess for additional injuries when either injury is identified, thereby improving comprehensive patient evaluation and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 1184
retrieved_docs.content: effects as specific conditions associated with the concept of “كسر,” situates them within a broader category of unspecified injury sequelae, and further delineates limb‑specific fracture sequelae, thereby providing a structured framework for understanding and managing the long‑term consequences of various bone injuries.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2431
retrieved_docs.content: Cervical shortening in pregnancy is categorized as a component of other complications of birth and puerperium that influence the management of the mother, and it specifically encompasses the sub‑condition of cervical shortening occurring in the second trimester.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 1 -- Community 208
retrieved_docs.content: Overall, the knowledge network unifies head and neck trauma, intracranial and skull‑base injuries, all skeletal fractures, their associated tissue damage, poisoning mechanisms, external causes (MVT, aircraft crash), procedural treatments, and the spectrum of late‑effect sequelae under the central concepts of fracture and late effects of unspecified injury, providing a comprehensive framework for injury classification, management, and long‑term patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2087
retrieved_docs.content: Head and neck injury is classified as a subset of إصابة في الرقبة, the Arabic term for neck injury, indicating that trauma involving the head and neck is considered part of the broader category of neck injuries. This relationship underscores that clinical evaluation and management of head and neck trauma are encompassed within the scope of neck injury assessment.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2082
retrieved_docs.content: Crushing injuries or internal injuries are associated with open wounds (جرح مفتوح), indicating that such traumatic forces often result in visible tissue disruption.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 2 -- Community 984
retrieved_docs.content: Fracture of cervical spine with cord lesion is identified as a كسر في الفقرة الرقبية, placing this specific injury within the broader category of cervical vertebral fractures. The كسر في الفقرة الرقبية is classified among other fractures, indicating that it shares common diagnostic and management considerations with a range of fracture types. It is also recognized as a subcategory of إصابة في الرقبة, linking cervical vertebral fractures to the overall spectrum of neck injuries. Clinically, كسر الحوض frequently co‑occurs with كسر في الفقرة الرقبية, highlighting the importance of evaluating patients for concurrent pelvic fractures when a cervical spine fracture is present. Understanding these hierarchical and associative relationships aids clinicians in comprehensive injury assessment, appropriate imaging, and coordinated treatment planning for patients with complex trauma involving the neck and pelvis.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 1 -- Community 166
retrieved_docs.content: legal interventions, cervical spine fractures, cutting/piercing mechanisms, and neurotoxic tissue damage through shared classifications, hierarchical inverse‑isa and cause‑of relationships, and common morphological outcomes such as تَلَف الأنسجة, providing clinicians with an integrated framework for coding, diagnosing, and managing a wide spectrum of injuries.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 2 -- Community 310
retrieved_docs.content: The late effect of fracture of multiple bones, the late effect of fracture of the spine and/or trunk without spinal cord lesion, the الآثار المتأخرة لكسر الأطراف السفلية (late effects of fracture of the lower extremities), the الآثار المتأخرة لكسر الأطراف العلوية (late effects of fracture of the upper extremities), and the الآثار المتأخرة لكسر عنق عظم الفخذ (late effects of fracture of the femoral neck) are all described as being associated with كسر (fracture). This indicates that delayed complications can arise after fractures affecting various skeletal regions, including multiple bones, the spine or trunk, the lower and upper limbs, and the femoral neck, and that these sequelae are intrinsically linked to the initial fracture event.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 1781
retrieved_docs.content: Crushing injury or internal injury is associated with the medical condition referred to as إصابة (injury), indicating that such traumatic forces commonly result in injury.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 2177
retrieved_docs.content: Fracture of cervical spine with cord lesion is classified as a كسر في الفقرة الرقبية (cervical vertebral fracture). In addition, كسر الحوض (pelvic fracture) frequently co‑occurs with كسر في الفقرة الرقبية, indicating that patients who sustain a cervical vertebral fracture often have concurrent pelvic injuries. This interrelationship underscores the importance of comprehensive assessment for both cervical and pelvic trauma in affected individuals.
